title: ExEF Capstone Transparency Rubric
body: Expert EF Capstone Transparency Rubric Certification Program · v2026.02 This sample rubric demonstrates how ExEF distinguishes Passing versus Needs Revision outcomes in capstone review. It is a transparency artifact and does not replace full reviewer scoring sheets. Component 1 Intake Simulation Rapport Building Passing Standard Uses primarily open-ended prompts and reflective listening. Validates client frustration without judgment. Maintains collaborative tone and role clarity. Needs Revision Uses primarily directive advice or closed questions. Dismisses client affect and jumps to fixing. Communicates in deficit-focused language. EF Explanation (Psychoeducation) Passing Standard Explains EF with accessible, non-clinical language. Uses process framing (e.g., air-traffic-control metaphor). Preserves strengths-based tone. Needs Revision Uses dense jargon without translation. Frames EF problems as character deficits. Omits practical behavior translation. Component 2 Intervention Plan Theoretical Grounding Passing Standard Intervention choices map explicitly to Barkley, Brown, Ward, or Dawson/Guare concepts. Rationale links each tactic to a documented EF mechanism. Needs Revision Suggestions are generic or motivational only. No model-to-intervention rationale is provided. Environmental Modification Passing Standard Includes concrete external supports before relying on willpower. Includes implementation details at point-of-performance. Needs Revision Relies on internal effort alone. Lacks operational environmental changes. Interpretation Notes A passing result requires acceptable performance across all required domains. High-variance or borderline submissions may be escalated to manual audit. Final release follows ExEF feedback release workflow.

title: ExEF Curriculum Standard Alignment Map
body: Expert EF Curriculum Standard Alignment Map Certification Program · v2026.02 This map shows how ExEF curriculum components align to ICF competency language and EF-specific professional standard themes. Crosswalk ExEF Curriculum to ICF Competency Crosswalk ExEF Module ICF Competency Standard Theme Module 1.1 Inhibition and the Pause Evokes Awareness Neuroscience foundation (PFC development, inhibition control) Module 2.2 Intake and Goal Setting Establishes Agreements Assessment practice using structured EF profiling workflows Module 3 Coaching Architecture Cultivates Trust and Safety Scope-of-practice boundaries and referral ethics Module 4 Applied Methodologies Facilitates Client Growth Intervention design and environmental engineering Module 6 Ethics and Practice Demonstrates Ethical Practice Professional conduct, privacy, and documentation quality * Module 5 does not have a dedicated ICF competency mapping in this version of the crosswalk. Evidence References ICF Core Competencies NBEFC competency and credentialing guidance Barkley (1997) inhibition and executive-function framework Status Note This crosswalk is an internal alignment artifact and not a formal external accreditation claim.

title: About | Expert EF
body: Skip to main content Institute Profile About Expert EF ExEF is built as a public-facing institute file: model language, teaching logic, and operating boundaries are visible before any reviewed service is offered. This page explains what ExEF is, what intellectual traditions it draws from, and how the institute distinguishes open educational material from paid review and credential work. At A Glance Model Open education with paid competency review Orientation Research-literate, ethically bounded, practice-facing Leadership Founder-led with public rubric and crosswalk artifacts What This Page Does Defines ExEF as an institute, not a generic coaching brand Primary Use Inspect the models, worldview, and teaching posture behind the curriculum Best Next Step Move to Curriculum for the full program file or Certification for standards Institute Thesis Training coaches to function as an external scaffold ExEF sits at the intersection of education, coaching practice, and applied executive-function support, but it organizes those domains into a named teaching system rather than a loose collection of advice. As awareness of neurodiverse profiles — specifically ADHD and Autism Spectrum Disorder — expands beyond diagnosis into mainstream educational and workplace contexts, the demand for specialized executive-function support has grown sharply. Yet the field remains largely unregulated, with a significant gap between the quality of available research and the rigor of practitioner training. Our internal certification pathway is built on the synthesis of multiple theoretical models into a single, actionable coaching framework — one designed to be research-literate, ethically bounded, and practically useful. Core Philosophy "Executive function deficits are rarely deficits of knowing; they are deficits of doing. The role of the coach is not merely to teach a client how to use a planner, but to act as an external scaffold while training the client's own neural ci

title: Guide: Barkley's Inhibition Model | ExEF
body: Skip to content A Guide to Barkley's Inhibition Model A practical interpretation of inhibition-first executive function science for educators and coaches. Three Stages of Inhibition 1. Stop Pause prepotent response 2. Interrupt Break in-progress action chain 3. Protect Shield goal from interference This sequence helps explain why students can "know what to do" yet still struggle in point-of-performance contexts. Coaching makes inhibition supports more visible and repeatable. Translate to Interventions Compare to Brown Model Further Sources: Barkley Citations 30 Essential Ideas 1B 30 Essential Ideas 5A 30 Essential Ideas 7B 30 Essential Ideas 9C 30 Essential Ideas 10E Open citations and source trails

title: Barkley vs Brown: Comparative Guide | ExEF
body: Skip to content Barkley vs Brown In EFI's teaching use, Barkley is most useful for mechanism and Brown for symptom-pattern description, then interventions can draw from both views. Criterion Barkley Brown Core Question Why control fails in the moment How difficulties cluster in daily life Clinical Use Mechanism-level explanatory model Profile-level descriptive model Coaching Strength Designing inhibition supports Prioritizing intervention domains Common Limitation In Coaching Use Can feel less descriptive for lived profile detail Can be less explicit about inhibitory sequencing Run Brown Checklist Apply in Certification Cases

title: Brown's 6 Clusters Pre-Diagnostic Tool | ExEF
body: Skip to content Brown's 6 Clusters: Pre-Diagnostic Checklist This is an educational screener for pattern recognition, not a diagnosis. Brown's six-cluster map for conversation structuring and intervention targeting. Rate Struggle Level Select one statement per cluster. 1. Activation (starting/organizing) Minimal Mild Moderate High 2. Focus Minimal Mild Moderate High 3. Effort Minimal Mild Moderate High 4. Emotion Minimal Mild Moderate High 5. Memory Minimal Mild Moderate High 6. Action Monitoring Minimal Mild Moderate High Generate Pattern Summary Map to Interventions Train as Certified Coach After You Score Use your top two clusters to choose one environmental intervention and one skill-building intervention for the next seven days. Further Sources: Brown Citations CHADD article: six aspects of executive function Brown EF model summary Brown EF/A scales overview handout Open citations and source trails

title: Coach Directory Policy | ExEF
body: Skip to content Coach Directory Policy Last updated: February 17, 2026 This policy defines how directory listings are published, reviewed, and maintained. Eligibility Requirements All six curriculum modules passed. Capstone passed under published rubric criteria. Scope-safe profile language (coaching only, no therapy claims). Valid location and service modality details. Moderation and Verification Status Active: Verified and reviewed within the last 180 days. Probation: Verified but review age exceeds 180 days. Expired: Verified status is older than 365 days; listing may be hidden until re-review. Pending: Submitted and awaiting moderation/verification. Rejected: Did not meet listing or scope requirements. Archived: Removed from public display by reviewer/admin action. Update and Correction Requests Listing owners may request profile updates via info@theexecutivefunctioninginstitute.com . ExEF may request supporting verification information before updates are published. Consent and Data Use Submitting a directory request grants ExEF permission to publish professional profile data in the public directory. ExEF does not publish private billing, full assessment, or private session records in directory listings. Back to Directory

title: Contact | ExEF Coaching Practice
body: Skip to main content Consultation & Intake Desk Whether you're ready to start creative EF coaching, ask about certification, or request a professional review, ExEF can route you to the right next step without making you decode the service menu first. Before You Reach Out The first conversation is for fit, not pressure Coaching, workshops, and school-support requests are routed differently Most messages get a direct reply within 24-48 hours Choose the Right Entry Point The cleanest intake starts by matching the request to the right lane instead of treating everything like a generic contact form. Free Consultation Call The best way to start. A 20-minute call to review your goals and decide whether creative EF coaching, certification, or a professional review is the right fit. No cost, no obligation Learn how the ExEF coaching or certification path works Ask questions about pricing, fit, and timing Get a recommendation on next steps Book Your Free Call Send a Message Have a quick question or prefer to communicate in writing? Use the form below and ExEF will follow up within 24-48 hours. Workshop Registration Want to sign up for a Saturday Brain Games workshop? Use the form to request upcoming dates and reserve a spot. Routing rule If you are unsure, choose the consultation call. It is the fastest way to figure out whether the right next step is 1:1 coaching, a workshop format, school-support help, or something else. What Helps Most Student age or grade The main friction point right now What you have already tried Whether you need coaching, workshop info, or school translation Send a Message Tell ExEF a bit about your situation and the support you are looking for. This form works best when the goal is clarity, not a perfect writeup. Your Name * Email Address * Phone Number Student's Age/Grade Service Area * Select an option... Free Consultation Call CEFC Enrollment / Certification Inquiry 1:1 Executive Functioning Coaching Saturday Brain Games Workshop IEP Translation & Adv

title: EFI Coaching Practice | Executive Functioning Support San Diego
body: Skip to main content EFI Coaching | San Diego Creative Executive Function Coaching That Actually Engages Your Child EFI combines executive functioning expertise, school-system fluency, and creative performance tools (improv, chess, and cubing) to build focus, flexibility, and self-regulation through methods students genuinely engage with. Schedule a Consultation Start With Free Tools If you are still figuring out whether the issue is executive function, start with the free assessment desk first. Use consultation when you already know you want local support. Special Education Background Improv & Comedy Trained Chess & Cubing Specialist Practice Snapshot Primary Client Base Neurodivergent students, families, and school-transition cases Working Style Creative-to-structured coaching: play-based drills, practical planning, and school-context translation Local Footprint San Diego sessions, workshops, and parent support around district friction points Good Fit Indicators Creative, high-potential student with inconsistent execution Tutoring has become another fight Family needs school-system translation Engagement matters as much as rigor Image Slot 5 Practice image Use for a real coaching session, desk setup, or hands-on EF activity photograph. Recommended ratio: portrait or square image with strong human presence. What Families Usually Bring to the First Call The issue is rarely raw intelligence. It is usually a translation gap between a student's creative strengths and reliable daily execution at home, in class, and under stress. "They're so smart, but..." Your child aces tests when engaged but "forgets" to turn in homework. Their potential is obvious—so why isn't it showing up? Tutoring feels like punishment You've tried tutors, but sitting through more "school after school" creates resistance, not results. The IEP maze is overwhelming "Preferential seating" and "extended time" sound helpful—but you're not sure your child is getting what they actually need. Executive Fu

title: Curriculum | Expert EF
body: Skip to main content Program File The Certification Curriculum This is the public syllabus file for EFI's reviewed pathway. It shows the teaching order, the assignment logic, and the specific frameworks the institute expects people to work from. Use this page to inspect substance before looking at pricing. The sequence is designed to move from mechanism to assessment to intervention to professional judgment. Inspect Grading Rubric View Paid Path Curriculum Dossier Format Asynchronous study + applied assignments Access Open curriculum pages, paid scoring and credential operations Sequence Theory, assessment, coaching architecture, interventions, practice launch Capstone Launch kit, competency review, rubric-based evaluation Representative capstone file stack used later in the reviewed workflow. 1 Neuropsychology 2 Assessment 3 Coaching Architecture 4 Applied Methods 5 Interventions 6 Professional Practice Public File Shows the full syllabus and assignment spine before any grading workflow starts Review Boundary Reading is open. Scoring, evaluator feedback, and credential decisions are paid review operations. Best Use Open a module page, the rubric, and the capstone preview in that order if you need proof instead of marketing copy. Sequence Memo Why the syllabus is ordered this way EFI treats executive function coaching as a sequence of judgments. The curriculum starts with mechanism, then moves into assessment, intervention architecture, applied tools, and finally the ethical and operational demands of practice. The point of the order is not polish. It is defensibility. Coaches should understand why a tool fits before they recommend it, and they should understand scope before they offer it professionally. Theory comes first so coaching choices can be defended against named research frameworks rather than generic advice. Assessment comes next so goals are built from functional data, discrepancies, and point-of-performance reality. Architecture and methods follow so in

Back to Assessment Hub Scale 1 = Strongly disagree and 5 = Strongly agree . These statements describe friction patterns, so higher scores generally indicate more daily drag in that area. 0 of 12 answered Generate My Profile Story Reset Your Result Profile Title Your Profile Narrative Where You Get Stuck What Already Works Your 7-Day Experiment Dimension Friction Scores Copy Summary Export Text Generate a profile to enable export actions. title: Free Executive Functioning Test (ESQ-R) | ExEF
body: Skip to main content Free ESQ-R Interactive Intake EFI's live ESQ-R tool now matches the site's current public model: 25 items , 5 executive-function areas , and one result flow designed to connect cleanly with the rest of the EFI assessment suite. Back to Assessment Hub Open Cross-Signal Profile What This Measures Planning and time management, task initiation and follow-through, attention and working memory, organization and self-monitoring, and emotional regulation and flexibility. How To Answer Rate each statement from 1 to 5 based on how you usually function, not on your best day. Higher scores mean stronger current capacity in that area. What You Get A clearer result narrative, two current strengths, two leverage points, and a recommended path into the other EFI tools. Important framing This tool is for self-awareness, planning, and coaching-style reflection. It is not a diagnosis and should not be treated as one. 0 of 25 answered Please answer all 25 questions before generating your profile. Generate My ESQ-R Profile Reset Your Result Your ESQ-R profile Profile Snapshot Cross-Signal Read Current strengths These are the areas already giving you usable structure or recovery capacity. Main leverage points These lower-scoring areas are likely creating the most drag right now. Recommended next tools Save My Results + Receive EFI Tools Enter your email to save this summary and receive related EFI tools, planning resources, and occasional updates. Name Email I agree to receive EFI resources, updates, and occasional offers. I can unsubscribe anytime. Save + Subscribe Copy Summary Export Text Download PNG Download PDF Share Summary Continue the sequence ESQ-R gives you a broad intake pattern. EFI becomes more useful when you connect it to a situation-level tool or the full Cross-Signal Profile. Run Task Start Friction Open Time Calibrator Open Cross-Signal Profile Using ESQ-R In Professional Training ExEF teaches ESQ-R interpretation inside a broader intake and coaching

title: Executive Functioning Assessment Hub | ExEF
body: Skip to main content Executive Functioning Assessment Hub Start here if you want a clear route through EFI's free diagnostics. Use ESQ-R as the broad intake first, then add narrower tools only if you need a sharper read. Start With ESQ-R See The Full Sequence Diagnostic Stack Choose One Tool, or Complete the Full Sequence Each free tool targets a different layer of executive functioning. Completing multiple tools unlocks stronger pattern detection. ESQ-R Executive Functioning Test 25-item executive-function intake across five areas. Use this first when you want a broad intake pattern before moving into narrower diagnostics. Take ESQ-R EF Profile Story Quiz Narrative profile identifying your top friction dimensions with an immediate 7-day experiment plan. Use this after ESQ-R if the broad pattern needs more context. Take EF Profile Story Time Blindness Calibrator Compare estimated vs. actual durations to generate your personal planning correction factor. Open Calibrator Task Start Friction Diagnostic Rate common blockers and generate a rapid start protocol for the next task. Run Diagnostic Want One Combined Result? After ESQ-R plus one or two narrower tools, use the EFI Cross-Signal Profile to merge your diagnostic signals into one list of priorities, strengths, and a unified 7-day plan. Open Cross-Signal Profile Browse All Resources How It Works Build Your Full Profile in 3 Steps Step 1 Take the ESQ-R first to get a broad intake across the full skill set. Step 2 Add EF Profile Story, Time Blindness, or Task Start Friction to sharpen the pattern in a specific context. Step 3 Open your Cross-Signal Profile to merge all results into one practical 7-day plan. FAQ Frequently Asked Questions Are these executive functioning tests free? + Yes. The ESQ-R, EF Profile Story quiz, Time Blindness Calibrator, and Task Start Friction diagnostic are available at no cost on the site. Do I need to complete every diagnostic? + No. You can take any tool individually. If you complete at

Four Cross-Signal Domains Planning, Activation, Regulation, and Environment. Output One profile frame, one leverage point, and one 7-day action plan. title: Expert EF | Science-Based EF Coaching Education
body: Skip to main content Institute Field Guide Public executive function coursework with visible standards EFI is built so visitors can inspect the teaching system before they ever hit a checkout page: the six-module syllabus, the named research models, the free assessment desk, and the capstone review boundary. Inspect the Curriculum Start With a Free Assessment What is public: module pages, framework maps, downloadable tools, and credential boundaries. What is paid: reviewer labor, graded submissions, evaluator feedback, and certificate operations. Best first move: use one free diagnostic or one proof page before comparing any paid path. Frameworks taught Barkley, Brown, Dawson & Guare, Ward The coursework is built around named models, not abstract productivity language. Open proof layer Curriculum, tools, directories, and standards Visitors can inspect the actual teaching surfaces before any reviewer-led service begins. Reviewed boundary Scoring, capstone review, and credential operations Paid work starts where manual evaluation and human judgment enter the process. Evidence Dossier The first screen should show proof, not a promise Primary file Six-module public syllabus Scoring logic Capstone transparency rubric Competency map Crosswalk of standards and outputs Open tools Assessment desk and public downloads Proof Surfaces Read the first module like a real course page Inspect standards and rubric language Browse the open EF resources directory Workflow Artifact This workflow is published so visitors can inspect where open study ends and reviewed credential labor begins. Reading Order Use the site like a case file, not a brochure EFI is easier to trust when visitors read one proof surface first, then move into the route that matches their work. The sequence below keeps the homepage directional instead of decorative. Recommended Order 1 Read one proof surface Open the syllabus, rubric, or a module page before you compare any paid option. title: Module 1: Neuropsychology of Self-Regulation | Expert EF
body: Skip to main content Module 1: Neuropsychology of Self-Regulation Establishing the intellectual baseline for the certification — synthesizing competing yet complementary theories of executive dysfunction into a cohesive coaching philosophy. Module Overview The Theoretical Foundation This module establishes the intellectual baseline for the certification. Trainees synthesize competing yet complementary theories of executive dysfunction to form a cohesive coaching philosophy. Why This Module Matters Before a coach can intervene, they must understand the mechanism of the deficit. Module 1 answers the fundamental question: Why does my client struggle with things that seem "easy" to others? The answer lies not in willpower or character, but in the neurobiology of the prefrontal cortex and the developmental trajectory of self-regulation. Unit 1.1 The Neurobiology of the "Air Traffic Control" System Harvard's Center on the Developing Child provides the foundational metaphor: the brain's executive functions operate like an air traffic control system, managing the flow of information and coordinating complex operations. The Harvard Metaphor Just as an airport's air traffic control system manages multiple planes arriving and departing simultaneously, the brain's executive function system manages the flow of information, prioritizes tasks, and enables us to filter distractions, hold plans in mind, and switch gears when necessary. This metaphor helps clients and families quickly reframe the issue: not a "lazy pilot," but an overloaded control tower . Key Insight The prefrontal cortex — the brain's "control tower" — is among the last brain regions to reach mature function, continuing development from infancy into early adulthood . This extended developmental timeline has profound implications for coaching adolescents and young adults. The Adolescent "Gap" Period During adolescence, a critical "gap" emerges: the limbic system (emotion and reward centers) matures rapidly, while th

title: Module 2: Assessment & Intake Strategy | Expert EF
body: Skip to main content 2 Assessment Protocols & Intake Strategy Transitioning from theory to diagnostics. Learn to assess functional impairment through structured interviews, self-report questionnaires, and performance audits. Module Overview While coaches do not diagnose pathology, they must assess functional impairment to tailor their interventions. This module relies heavily on the work of Dawson and Guare to structure the assessment process. Key Distinction Coaches assess functional impairment , not clinical pathology. The goal is not diagnosis but understanding how the client's executive skills interact with their environmental demands — what Dawson and Guare call the "Goodness of Fit." Unit 2.1 The Intake Architecture The intake session is the most critical hour of the coaching engagement. Data Gathering Structured questionnaires to identify the client's history, interests, strengths, and challenge areas. Look for patterns of "strong starts and poor finishes." Rapport Building Creating a "shame-free zone" where missed tasks are data points for problem-solving, not moral failings. Establishing the collaborative alliance. Goodness of Fit Problems arise when the demands of the environment exceed the client's executive skills. The intake must identify both the skill deficits and the environmental demands. The "Getting to Know You" Protocol An effective intake uses a structured questionnaire to probe key areas: Academic/Career History Looking for patterns of "strong starts and poor finishes" or specific areas of difficulty that point to underlying EF deficits. Bio-Regulatory Factors Sleep, nutrition, and exercise. Research indicates that EF reserves are depleted by fatigue and stress — these must be assessed first. Islands of Competence Where does the client succeed? (e.g., video games, sports, art). This reveals that EF is context-dependent, not absent. Unit 2.2 The Executive Skills Questionnaire (ESQ-R) The ESQ-R is the Institute's primary public-facing intake surv

title: Module 3: The Coaching Architecture | Expert EF
body: Skip to main content 3 The Coaching Architecture Operationalizing the Dawson & Guare intervention framework. Distinguishing coaching from tutoring and therapy, and mastering the art of environmental modification and skill acquisition. Module Overview This module operationalizes the intervention process. It distinguishes EF coaching from tutoring (which focuses on content) and therapy (which focuses on pathology), framing coaching as a process of "skill acquisition" and "environmental modification." The Core Metaphor The coach initially serves as the client's "external frontal lobe" or surrogate executive system. This involves lending executive skills while simultaneously fostering independence. Critical to this model is the plan for obsolescence — from session one, the coach must plan how to transfer these functions back to the client. Unit 3.1 The Two-Tiered Intervention Logic Before asking the client to change, the coach must examine the external context. Tier 1: Change the Environment Before asking the client to change, examine the external context. Can the task be shortened? Can the workspace be altered? Can external cues (alarms, visual schedules) be introduced? This aligns with Barkley's "Extended Phenotype" theory — offloading executive demand onto the physical environment. Shorten or restructure the task Modify the physical workspace Introduce external cues and reminders Reduce environmental distractions Add visual schedules and checklists at the "point of performance" Tier 2/3: Teach the Skill If environmental modification is insufficient, the coach teaches internal strategies. This involves explicit instruction, modeling, and rehearsal. The curriculum emphasizes that telling a client what to do is ineffective ; they must practice the "how" during the session. Explicit instruction of the strategy Coach modeling of the behavior Guided rehearsal during the session Practice in the natural environment Review, adjust, and generalize Unit 3.2 The Coach as "Extern

title: Module 4: Applied Methodologies | Expert EF
body: Skip to main content 4 Applied Methodologies Concrete tools for time and task management using the "360 Thinking" model, analog temporal strategies, and cognitive offloading techniques. Module Overview To provide concrete tools for time and task management, this module incorporates the "360 Thinking" model developed by Sarah Ward and Kristen Jacobsen. It moves beyond general lists to specific visual-spatial strategies. Guiding Philosophy This module addresses the most practical question in EF coaching: "How do I actually help someone who can't start, can't estimate time, and can't stay organized?" The answers lie in externalization — making the invisible visible. Unit 4.1 360 Thinking: "Get Ready, Do, Done" The primary methodology: planning backwards to execute forwards. Step 1: Done Visualize the final outcome first . "What will it look like when I am finished?" This utilizes the nonverbal working memory (visual imagery) identified by Barkley. The client creates a mental photograph of the completed task. If they're packing for a trip, they visualize themselves at the airport with everything they need. Step 2: Do Once the end state is visualized, identify the specific action steps required. Map the work backward from the finished product to the present moment. Break down the gap between "now" and "done" into concrete, observable steps. Each step should be small enough to not trigger anxiety. Step 3: Get Ready Finally, determine what materials are needed to start. This reverses the typical impulsive approach of grabbing materials without a plan. Use Red/Green/Yellow planning mats to visually organize this process. Red = not ready, Yellow = getting ready, Green = go. Unit 4.2 Temporal Management: Addressing "Time Blindness" Addressing the inability to "feel" or "see" the passage of time. The Problem Clients with EF challenges often perceive time as a nebulous concept. They struggle to "feel" the passage of time or estimate how long tasks take. Barkley calls this "temp

title: Module 5: Strategic Interventions & Special Populations | Expert EF
body: Skip to main content 5 Strategic Interventions & Special Populations The practitioner's toolkit: specific "how-to" strategies for remediating executive dysfunction, plus adaptations for ADHD, ASD, and critical life transitions. Module Overview This module constitutes the "toolkit" of the practitioner. It transitions from theory to the specific strategies for remediating executive dysfunction. The guiding philosophy is Environmental Engineering : changing the environment to modify behavior. Unit 5.1 Time Management Across Populations Population-Specific Barriers Module 4 introduced time visualization tools and the prediction exercise. Here, the focus shifts to why the same tools fail for different populations and how coaches adapt temporal interventions accordingly. Adaptations by Population Adolescents: Resistance to “childish” tools — use phone-native timers and app-based time blocking that feel age-appropriate College Students: Fluid schedules with no external bell system — teach weekly architecture reviews, not daily to-do lists Adults with Late Diagnosis: Deep shame around chronic lateness — reframe the Time Correction Factor as a calibration tool, not evidence of failure Parents with EF Challenges: Competing temporal demands from children — build family-level visual schedules that serve the parent and child simultaneously Example: The Prediction Exercise Visual trend: predicted times cluster below actual durations, generating a personal correction multiplier. Task Predicted Actual Factor Shower 10 min 25 min 2.5x Pack bag 5 min 15 min 3x Write email 10 min 30 min 3x Math hw 30 min 60 min 2x This data reveals a consistent pattern: the client underestimates by 2–3x. This becomes their personal "Time Correction Factor" for future planning. Unit 5.2 Task Initiation: Overcoming the "Wall of Awful" The Deficit Procrastination is often an emotional regulation issue, not a laziness issue. The task has become associated with negative emotions (fear of failure, boredom),

title: Module 6: Professional Ethics & Practice Management | Expert EF
body: Skip to main content 6 Professional Ethics & Practice Management Operating within legal and ethical boundaries while building a sustainable, impactful coaching practice. Module Overview The final module ensures that graduates operate within legal and ethical boundaries and possess the business acumen to build a sustainable practice. Even the most skilled coach cannot help clients if they cannot attract them or manage the logistics. The Path Forward Completing this curriculum marks the start of practice development. The open-source model expects ongoing learning from new research, tools, and field feedback. Unit 6.1 Professional Ethics & Scope of Practice Aligning with the ICF and NBEFC codes of ethics. ICF & NBEFC Alignment The curriculum aligns with the International Coaching Federation (ICF) and National Board of Executive Function Certification (NBEFC) codes of ethics. Competence + Coaches shall provide services only within the boundaries of their education, training, and experience. For example, referral to therapists for trauma or comorbidities is required when issues exceed the scope of EF coaching. Confidentiality + Information shared in coaching is strictly confidential unless there is an imminent risk of harm to self or others. With minor clients, the "Triangle of Trust" dictates what is shared with parents. Integrity + Coaches shall not make false claims regarding outcomes (e.g., "curing" ADHD). Marketing must be honest and evidence-based. Conflict of Interest + Coaches must disclose any financial interest in products or services recommended to clients. Scope of Practice Coaches must be trained to recognize red flags that require referral to clinical mental health professionals: Referral Red Flags Severe depression or suicidal ideation Substance abuse or dependency Trauma responses (PTSD, complex trauma) Significant anxiety disorders requiring clinical intervention Eating disorders or self-harm behaviors Psychotic symptoms or severe thought disturbance The

title: Privacy Policy | Expert EF
body: Skip to content Privacy Policy Effective date: February 16, 2026 Expert EF (ExEF) collects and processes personal data only to operate the website, deliver requested resources, and provide paid certification/coaching services. What We Collect Account data: name, email, login/session data, role metadata. Learning data: module progress, ESQ-R outputs, submission metadata, scoring/feedback records. Commerce data: order records, product entitlements, credential IDs, fulfillment status. Operational telemetry: page events, referrer, browser/device diagnostics, error events. How We Use Data Provide account access, progress tracking, and dashboard features. Deliver paid services, including assessment/report outputs and credential verification. Send consented communications, updates, and resource offers. Detect abuse, secure systems, and troubleshoot service issues. Payments and Authentication Payment processing is handled by third-party processors. ExEF does not store full payment card numbers. Purchase fulfillment is released only after server-side verification. Authentication and authorization are server-backed. Browser-only role data is not considered authoritative for privileged actions. Data Sharing ExEF may share consented lead/event data with configured CRM and email service providers to deliver requested resources and communications. ExEF does not sell personal data. Implementation-Specific Data Paths Primary application storage: Supabase (PostgreSQL) for progress, purchases, submissions, leads, events, and directory records. Server execution layer: Netlify Functions for API processing, signature verification, and webhook handling. Payment verification: Stripe webhook event metadata used for entitlement release logic. AI grading services: Gemini API requests include assignment content and rubric prompts for scoring outputs. Retention Account and purchase records: retained while account is active, then archived for compliance and fraud preventio

title: Coaching vs Therapy Scope of Practice | ExEF
body: Skip to content Coaching vs Therapy: Scope of Practice Operational boundaries, non-diagnostic language, referral triggers, and ethical limits for ExEF coaching practice. Area EF Coaching Therapy / Clinical Care Primary Aim Skill building + implementation systems Mental health diagnosis/treatment Primary Method Planning, routines, accountability design Clinical interventions, psychotherapy protocols Assessment Role Educational and coaching-oriented interpretation Clinical evaluation and diagnosis School Support Role Preparation, parent education, accommodation translation Legal representation or licensed advocacy where required Referral Trigger Persistent safety concerns or clinical symptom escalation Appropriate intake into treatment pathway Critical Practice Rule When risk of harm, severe mood instability, active trauma symptoms, or other urgent mental-health concerns are present, pause coaching scope and refer to licensed clinical providers or emergency resources. ExEF Does Not Represent Coaching As Medical or psychological diagnosis Psychotherapy or mental-health treatment Legal advice or attorney representation Guaranteed educational, financial, or clinical outcomes ExEF Coaching May Include Planning systems and accountability design Parent preparation for school meetings Accommodation translation in plain language Referral out when client needs exceed coaching scope Standards + Accreditation Ethics Module

title: Reviewed Services | Expert EF
body: Skip to main content Reviewed Services & Professional Review EFI keeps the curriculum free and charges for the part that actually requires expert labor: human grading, practicum review, credential decisions, and professional interpretation. Use this page after the route is already clear. Parents usually arrive here from assessments or coaching, educators from the transition page, and practitioners from certification standards and curriculum artifacts. Pricing Logic EFI sells reviewer labor, not access theater The educational layer stays open. The paid layer begins where a real person has to score, audit, interpret, or credential the work. Free Layer Curriculum pages, reading structure, open tools, source notes, and public standards. Paid Layer Human-scored assignments, professional interpretation, capstone review, revision notes, and credential operations. Page Role This page is the public reviewed-services dossier so buyers can inspect scope before starting an intake conversation. At a glance Highest-Touch Offer CEFC reviewed pathway from enrollment through capstone decision Fastest Entry ESQ-R professional analysis for families or adults who want interpretation first Current Purchase Model Public pricing with account-based purchase records; some services may still begin with intake or consultation Check Your Route Review Standards First Route Check Which reviewed path are you actually choosing? The store should confirm a decision, not force visitors to make one from scratch. If you cannot name your role and desired outcome yet, leave this page and go back to the start paths. Too Early? If you are still trying to understand the problem, use the free assessment desk. If you teach and are exploring a transition, use the educator route. If you already know the standards fit, then use this page to compare reviewer-led services. Parent or Adult Seeking Interpretation Use this route if you want expert interpretation before deciding on ongoing support. The free assessment

title: Task Start Friction Diagnostic | ExEF
body: Skip to main content Task Start Friction Diagnostic Pick a real situation, rate the friction layers that are getting in the way, and ExEF will build a tailored start script you can use immediately. Best Use Use this when the problem is not knowing what matters, but actually starting. The diagnostic works best when you apply it to one real task, not a vague category. Choose A Situation Scenario A report due tomorrow Studying for a test you have been putting off Dealing with a missing assignment An important email you need to send Starting a proposal, project plan, or presentation Making a call you do not want to make Resetting a messy room or workspace Paying a bill or handling a money admin task Booking an appointment you have delayed A form or application you have been avoiding Choose a real scenario and ExEF will tailor the friction prompts and first-step protocol. Rate The Friction Layers Build Start Script Reset Copy Protocol Export Text No protocol copied yet. Your Start Protocol Rate each friction layer and EFI will build a tailored start script for this situation. Next Step In The Assessment Sequence Pair this start-friction result with ESQ-R, EF Profile Story, or Time Blindness data when you want ExEF to name the bigger pattern instead of a single moment. Open Time Blindness Calibrator Open Cross-Signal Model

title: Terms of Service | Expert EF
body: Skip to content Terms of Service These terms govern use of the Expert EF (ExEF) website, assessments, certification services, and coaching offers. Service Scope EFI provides educational and coaching-related services, not medical, legal, or crisis intervention services. Coaching is not therapy. Users are responsible for seeking licensed clinical care when needed. Do not upload protected clinical records or third-party confidential data without authorization. Program Delivery Model EFI certification delivery is asynchronous. EFI does not advertise required synchronous classes, required live lectures, or required group projects as part of core completion. Certification and Review Certification decisions are based on published rubric criteria and EFI quality-control workflows. EFI may delay release of feedback/results to support review integrity, including structured release windows after grading. Payments, Refunds, and Fulfillment Paid services are fulfilled only after server-side payment verification. EFI offers a full refund within 30 calendar days of purchase. Physical fulfillment items may be eligible for replacement if damaged in transit; report fulfillment issues within 14 days of delivery. Unauthorized chargebacks or abuse of access controls may result in account suspension pending review. Operational Review Timelines EFI uses a delayed-release review workflow for consistency and quality control. Typical windows are published on certification and store pages and may vary during high-volume periods. Marketing and Earnings Disclaimer Examples, calculators, and planning tools are illustrative and do not guarantee income, client volume, or business outcomes. Similarly, case examples, client feedback excerpts, and session outcome descriptions on coaching and marketing pages represent selected experiences and do not guarantee equivalent educational, behavioral, or learning outcomes for any individual. Results vary based on student profile, consist

title: Time Blindness Calibrator | ExEF
body: Skip to main content Time Blindness Calibrator Compare your first-draft time estimates against realistic ExEF planning benchmarks. After a few entries, the tool calculates your timing drift, confidence band, and a correction factor you can reuse this week. Best Use Use this after ESQ-R or alongside it if planning and time realism are major friction points. Add at least three entries for a usable pattern. Add A Benchmark Check Context Choose a context... Task Choose a context first... Your Estimate (minutes) Task Complexity Easier than usual About typical Harder than usual How confident are you in that estimate? Low confidence Moderate confidence High confidence ExEF Benchmark Note Choose a context and task to see ExEF's typical time benchmark. Save Entry Reset Tool Copy Summary Export Text Export CSV No entries yet. Try 3 to 5 tasks and the tool will start showing how your internal timing tends to drift. Pattern Summary Your timing pattern will start to show after the first entry. Confidence: unavailable (add entries) Reusable Planning Summary Your planning summary will appear here once you have data. The credential ID is used as an optional cross-check. How this verification works Use the full verification link from the certificate when possible. Paste the signed receipt token if you were given the token separately. Enter the credential ID to confirm the token matches the displayed certificate.
